name: Nonstick Cookware (PTFE-coated pans)
description: Pans and pots coated with a fluoropolymer (PTFE/Teflon) nonstick layer. The coating can degrade, scratch, or release fumes when overheated.

hazards.rationale: The nonstick coating is PTFE; flakes and overheating fumes are the direct exposure route.
PTFE is a PFAS; legacy manufacturing and some coatings carry related per-/polyfluorinated residues.

detection_hints: Manufacturer warnings against high heat / empty preheating

name: Plastic Food Storage Container
description: Reusable plastic tubs for food storage, often #5 PP or #7 'other'. Heat and fatty foods can promote chemical migration, especially when microwaved.

hazards.rationale: Older or #7 polycarbonate containers can leach BPA; many are now BPA-free.
Plasticizers can be present in some flexible plastics and lids.
Wear, scratching, and microwaving shed micro/nanoplastic particles into food.

detection_hints: Recycling code #7 OTHER or #5 PP on base

sources.title: Bisphenol A (BPA): Use in Food Contact Application

name: Polycarbonate Reusable Bottle
description: Hard, clear reusable bottles made of polycarbonate, which is polymerized from BPA. BPA leaches with heat, age, and wear; recycling code #7.

hazards.rationale: Polycarbonate is synthesized from BPA monomer; hydrolysis releases BPA, accelerated by heat and scratching.
Surface wear and repeated washing shed particles.

sources.title: Bisphenol A (BPA): Use in Food Contact Application

name: Single-Use PET Water Bottle
description: Disposable beverage bottles made from PET (#1). Antimony is used as a polymerization catalyst and can leach, especially with heat and prolonged storage.

hazards.rationale: Antimony trioxide is the PET polymerization catalyst; trace leaching increases with heat and storage time.
PET bottles release micro/nanoplastics into the water they contain.

detection_hints: Bottles stored warm or reused repeatedly

name: Plastic Cutting Board
description: Polyethylene or polypropylene cutting boards. Knife abrasion sheds large numbers of microplastic particles directly onto food.

hazards.rationale: Repeated knife cuts abrade the surface, releasing micro/nanoplastics onto food being prepared.

detection_hints: Plastic (HDPE/PP) board with visible knife grooves

name: Plastic Utensils & Kitchen Tools
description: Plastic spatulas, spoons, and disposable cutlery, often polystyrene (#6) or polypropylene. Heat contact can promote styrene migration and particle shedding.

hazards.rationale: Polystyrene utensils can leach residual styrene monomer, especially with hot/fatty foods.
Heat exposure and wear from cooking shed plastic particles.

detection_hints: Melted or warped edges from heat

name: PVC Cling Film / Plastic Wrap
description: Flexible food wrap made from PVC plasticized with phthalates. Plasticizers migrate into fatty foods, especially with heat.

hazards.rationale: PVC film is softened with phthalate plasticizers that migrate into fatty foods.
DEHP has historically been a common PVC plasticizer in food-contact film.
Trace residual vinyl chloride monomer can persist in PVC products.

detection_hints: Cling film labeled PVC or recycling #3
Very clingy/stretchy wrap (vs #4 LDPE which is less clingy)

name: Lined Canned Food
description: Metal food/beverage cans with epoxy-based interior linings historically made with BPA. The liner is in direct contact with acidic and fatty foods.

hazards.rationale: Epoxy can linings are made from BPA; acidic/heated contents promote leaching.
BPA-free linings often substitute structurally similar bisphenols (BPS/BPF).

sources.title: Bisphenol A (BPA): Use in Food Contact Application

name: Plastic-Bodied Electric Kettle
description: Electric kettles with plastic interior water reservoirs or windows. Boiling water in contact with plastic promotes leaching and particle release.

hazards.rationale: Polycarbonate or other plastics in the water path can leach BPA at boiling temperatures.
Repeated boiling degrades plastic surfaces, shedding particles into hot water.

detection_hints: Plastic spout or lid contacting steam/water

name: Melamine Dishware
description: Hard, decorative plastic plates and bowls made from melamine-formaldehyde resin. Heat and acidic foods promote migration of melamine and formaldehyde.

hazards.rationale: Melamine-formaldehyde resin can release formaldehyde, especially with hot or acidic foods.

detection_hints: Lightweight but rigid, often colorful 'unbreakable' dishes

name: Nonstick Bakeware
description: Baking sheets, muffin tins, and pans with PTFE-based nonstick coatings. Oven heat and scratching degrade the fluoropolymer layer.

hazards.rationale: Dark nonstick coating is PTFE; high oven temperatures and scratches release particles/fumes.
Fluoropolymer coatings belong to the PFAS class and may carry processing-aid residues.

detection_hints: Dark, slick nonstick coating on bakeware

name: Single-Use Coffee Pods
description: Plastic or composite coffee capsules through which near-boiling water is forced. Hot water contact with plastic promotes leaching and particle release.

hazards.rationale: Hot pressurized water through plastic pods releases micro/nanoplastic particles into the brew.
Some plastic capsule components may carry plasticizer residues.

detection_hints: Brand pods designed for single-serve machines

name: Vinyl (PVC) Shower Curtain
description: Flexible plastic shower curtains/liners made from PVC plasticized with phthalates. The characteristic 'new curtain' smell is VOC and phthalate offgassing.

hazards.rationale: PVC curtains are softened with phthalate plasticizers that offgas and migrate.
New PVC curtains release volatile organic compounds (the distinctive plastic smell).
Trace residual vinyl chloride monomer can persist in PVC products.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Paraben-Preserved Cosmetics
description: Lotions, makeup, and creams preserved with parabens. Applied directly to skin, parabens absorb dermally and are common endocrine-disruptor concerns.

hazards.rationale: Methyl-/propyl-/butylparaben are widely used cosmetic preservatives absorbed through skin.

detection_hints: Ingredient list terms ending in '-paraben' (methylparaben, propylparaben)

name: Fragranced Personal Care Products
description: Scented lotions, body washes, deodorants, and perfumes where 'fragrance/parfum' undisclosed mixtures commonly contain phthalates as carriers/fixatives.

hazards.rationale: Phthalates (e.g., DEP) are commonly used in 'fragrance' to fix scent; disclosed only as 'parfum'.

name: Antibacterial Soap (Triclosan)
description: Antibacterial hand/body soaps historically formulated with triclosan, an antimicrobial endocrine-disruptor concern banned by FDA in OTC wash products (2016) but still found in some items.

hazards.rationale: Triclosan was the dominant antibacterial agent in these soaps; remains in some toothpastes/older stock.

sources.title: Antibacterial Soap? You Can Skip It

name: Plastic Toothbrush
description: Conventional toothbrushes with plastic handles and nylon bristles used daily in the mouth, with abrasion shedding microplastic particles.

hazards.rationale: Daily brushing abrades nylon bristles and plastic, a plausible route for microplastic ingestion.

detection_hints: Frayed/worn bristles after months of use

name: Conventional Nail Polish
description: Nail lacquers historically containing the 'toxic trio' — dibutyl phthalate, toluene, and formaldehyde — applied directly and offgassing VOCs during use.

hazards.rationale: Dibutyl phthalate (DBP) is a traditional plasticizer in nail polish for flexibility.
Formaldehyde/formaldehyde resin used as a hardener; '3-free' products omit it.
Solvents (toluene and others) offgas strongly during application and drying.

name: Aerosol Hairspray
description: Pressurized hair sprays that aerosolize solvents, propellants, and polymer fixatives, producing inhalable VOC exposure and sometimes phthalate fixatives.

hazards.rationale: Aerosol solvents and propellants release inhalable VOCs during spraying.
Phthalates can serve as fixatives/plasticizers in the polymer film and 'fragrance'.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Plastic Mesh Loofah / Bath Pouf
description: Synthetic mesh bath sponges made of polyethylene netting. Abrasion against skin and degradation in wet environments shed microplastics.

hazards.rationale: Polyethylene mesh fragments with use and washing, shedding microplastic particles down the drain and onto skin.

detection_hints: Synthetic plastic mesh pouf (not natural loofah gourd or sea sponge)
Fraying mesh after weeks of use

name: Fragranced All-Purpose Cleaner
description: Scented multi-surface spray cleaners emitting VOCs and undisclosed 'fragrance' mixtures that can include phthalates, with airborne exposure during use.

hazards.rationale: Solvents and fragrance compounds volatilize during spraying and surface drying.
'Fragrance' in cleaners may contain phthalate carriers.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Plug-In Air Freshener
description: Continuous-emission scented air fresheners that release fragrance VOCs and often phthalate carriers into indoor air over weeks.

hazards.rationale: Continuous heated/diffused fragrance releases a sustained stream of VOCs into indoor air.
Phthalates are commonly used as fragrance solvents/fixatives in air fresheners.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Antibacterial / Disinfectant Spray
description: Surface disinfectant sprays containing antimicrobials (historically triclosan in some, quats in others) plus solvents that release VOCs during use.

hazards.rationale: Triclosan appears in some antibacterial products, though many now use quaternary ammonium compounds.
Solvent and fragrance components volatilize during spraying.

detection_hints: 'Triclosan' or quaternary ammonium in active ingredients

sources.title: Antibacterial Soap? You Can Skip It
Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Fabric-Softener Dryer Sheets
description: Scented dryer sheets that coat laundry with fragrance and softening agents; heated drying releases VOCs through dryer vents and onto fabrics.

hazards.rationale: Heated drying volatilizes fragrance and softener chemicals, vented to air and deposited on clothing.
Fragrance in dryer sheets can contain phthalate carriers.

detection_hints: Scented dryer sheets / fabric softener products

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Flame-Retardant Mattress
description: Mattresses treated with chemical flame retardants to meet flammability standards; these can migrate into dust and are inhaled/ingested during sleep.

hazards.rationale: Older mattresses used PBDE flame retardants that persist and migrate into household dust.
Organophosphate ester flame retardants are common PBDE replacements in foam mattresses.

name: Memory Foam Mattress
description: Polyurethane memory-foam mattresses that offgas VOCs (the 'new mattress smell') and may carry organophosphate flame retardants.

hazards.rationale: Fresh polyurethane foam offgasses VOCs, the source of the strong 'new mattress' smell.
Foam mattresses often incorporate organophosphate ester flame retardants.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Polyester Bedding
description: Sheets and bedding made from PET polyester. Fibers shed microplastics through wear and laundering, and PET carries trace antimony catalyst residue.

hazards.rationale: Synthetic polyester fibers shed microfibers during use and washing.
Antimony trioxide is the PET polymerization catalyst and remains as trace residue in fibers.

detection_hints: '100% polyester' or polyester blend on care label

name: Vinyl (PVC) Flooring
description: PVC luxury-vinyl or sheet flooring plasticized with phthalates. It offgasses VOCs and sheds phthalate-laden dust over its lifetime.

hazards.rationale: PVC flooring is softened with phthalate plasticizers that migrate into house dust.
New vinyl flooring offgasses VOCs, especially shortly after installation.
Trace residual vinyl chloride monomer can persist in PVC products.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Synthetic Carpet
description: Nylon/polyester carpet with synthetic backing that offgasses VOCs when new, sheds microfibers, and can be treated with stain-resist PFAS coatings.

hazards.rationale: New carpet and adhesives offgas VOCs (the 'new carpet smell').
Stain-/water-resistant carpet treatments have historically used PFAS.
Synthetic carpet fibers abrade into microplastic-laden house dust.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Polyester Clothing
description: Garments made from PET polyester that shed microfibers during wear and laundering and carry trace antimony catalyst residue from PET production.

hazards.rationale: Synthetic garments shed large quantities of microfibers in every wash cycle.
Antimony trioxide PET catalyst remains as trace residue in polyester fibers.

name: Plastic Baby Bottle
description: Infant feeding bottles, historically polycarbonate (BPA) and now often polypropylene. Heating formula in plastic releases microplastics and can leach bisphenols.

hazards.rationale: Older polycarbonate baby bottles leach BPA; banned in many regions but legacy stock remains.
Heating and shaking formula in polypropylene bottles releases high counts of microplastic particles.

detection_hints: Recycling code (#7 PC older; #5 PP newer)

name: PVC Teether / Soft Plastic Baby Toy
description: Soft, chewable plastic teethers and toys made from PVC plasticized with phthalates, which infants mouth directly — a primary phthalate exposure route restricted by law.

hazards.rationale: Soft PVC is plasticized with phthalates; restricted in children's products but present in non-compliant/legacy items.
DEHP was a primary plasticizer in soft PVC toys before bans (US CPSIA, EU).

detection_hints: Soft, squeezable plastic teether (vs silicone/natural rubber)

name: Flame-Retardant Children's Pajamas
description: Children's sleepwear treated or constructed to meet flammability standards, historically using chemical flame retardants in direct skin contact during sleep.

hazards.rationale: Older treated sleepwear used brominated flame retardants; tight-fit polyester now common alternative.
Organophosphate ester treatments are used as PBDE replacements on some textiles.

name: PVC / Soft Plastic Children's Toys
description: Soft plastic toys and figures made from phthalate-plasticized PVC; some older/imported toys also carry lead or cadmium in pigments and stabilizers.

hazards.rationale: Soft PVC toys are plasticized with phthalates, mouthed and handled by children.
Lead has appeared in paints/stabilizers of older and imported toys.
Cadmium-based pigments and PVC stabilizers found in some imported toys/jewelry.

name: Fragranced Baby Wipes
description: Scented and preserved baby wipes applied to infant skin; 'fragrance' can carry phthalates and some formulations use formaldehyde-releasing or paraben preservatives.

hazards.rationale: Phthalate carriers can appear in 'fragrance' applied to sensitive infant skin.
Parabens are used as preservatives in some wipe formulations.
Formaldehyde-releasing preservatives are used in some wet wipes.

name: Thermal Receipt Paper
description: Cash-register and card receipts coated with a bisphenol developer (BPA or BPS) that transfers readily to skin on handling — a well-documented dermal exposure route.

hazards.rationale: BPA is the classic free-form color developer in thermal paper, readily absorbed through skin.
BPS commonly replaces BPA in 'BPA-free' thermal receipts as the developer.

detection_hints: Glossy, slick thermal receipt (darkens when scratched with a nail)
Receipts from registers, card terminals, ATMs

sources.title: Bisphenol A restriction in thermal paper

name: Scented Paraffin Candle
description: Petroleum-derived paraffin candles with synthetic fragrance that release VOCs and combustion byproducts into indoor air when burned.

hazards.rationale: Burning paraffin and synthetic fragrance emits VOCs and particulates into indoor air.
Fragrance oils can contain phthalates released during burning/evaporation.

sources.title: Volatile Organic Compounds' Impact on Indoor Air Quality

name: Plastic Storage Bin
description: Household storage totes and bins, typically polypropylene/polyethylene. A modest source of microplastic dust and, in some flexible/colored plastics, plasticizer residues.

hazards.rationale: Abrasion and UV/age degradation of plastic bins contributes to household microplastic dust.
Some flexible or pigmented plastics carry plasticizer residues.

detection_hints: Brittle, chalky surface on aged bins

name: Conventional Upholstered Sofa
description: Upholstered sofas with polyurethane foam cushions, historically treated with flame retardants and sometimes PFAS-based stain repellents; both migrate into house dust.

hazards.rationale: Pre-2015 polyurethane foam furniture frequently contained PBDE flame retardants that persist in dust.
Organophosphate ester flame retardants replaced PBDEs in foam furniture.
Stain-resistant upholstery treatments have historically used PFAS.

name: Plastic Dish Used in Microwave
description: Plastic plates, bowls, or containers heated in the microwave. Heat dramatically accelerates leaching of bisphenols, plasticizers, and microplastic release into food.

hazards.rationale: Microwaving plastic releases very high counts of micro/nanoplastics into the food.
Heat strongly accelerates BPA leaching from polycarbonate/#7 plastics.
Heat promotes migration of plasticizers from flexible plastics into food.

detection_hints: Heating food in plastic (recycling code on base)
Absence of or ignoring 'microwave safe' marking
